steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The students claim broken-wing display is exclusive to shorebirds (order Charadriiformes). To weaken this claim, the data must show birds from outside Charadriiformes performing the display.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'The common sandpiper (Charadriiformes) performs broken-wing display; the West Peruvian dove (Columbiformes) does not.': this shows a shorebird performing the display and a non-shorebird not performing it — consistent with the exclusivity claim, not against it. ✗
  • Choice B — 'Neither the West Peruvian dove nor the purple sandpiper is known to perform broken-wing display.': the purple sandpiper is a shorebird that does not perform the display — this complicates the claim but does not show non-shorebirds performing it. ✗
  • Choice C — 'Unlike the common sandpiper, the purple sandpiper does not perform broken-wing display, even though both are in Charadriiformes.': this shows variation within shorebirds, not the display occurring outside Charadriiformes. ✗
  • Choice D — 'Both the sooty grouse and the pine warbler are known to perform broken-wing display.': the pine warbler (Passeriformes) and sooty grouse (Galliformes) are both non-shorebirds that perform the display, directly contradicting the claim that it is exclusive to Charadriiformes. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
